screens.heading: Building a Vision
screens.subtitle: How to tell Claude exactly what you want

screens.sub: The difference between a frustrating tool and a brilliant assistant is how clearly you describe what you need.

left.label: Vague prompt
items: Claude thinks: Dashboard of what? For whom? What data? What style?
You get: a generic placeholder with fake numbers
Then you spend 10 minutes going back and forth

right.label: Specific prompt
items: "Build a sales dashboard showing our top 10 deals by value this quarter. Use the Oren dark theme. Show a bar chart and a summary card with total pipeline value."
Claude thinks: Got it. Bar chart, 10 deals, dark theme, summary card.
You get: something usable on the first try

screens.text: 30 extra seconds of thinking saves 10 minutes of back-and-forth.

items.title: What
items.description: What are you building? A page, a chart, a report, a tool? Name it.

items.title: Who
items.description: Who will use it? You, your team, a client? This changes everything about tone and layout.

items.title: Data
items.description: What information does it show? Real numbers, categories, dates? Be concrete.

items.title: Look
items.description: What should it look like? Dark theme, cards, charts, simple table? Reference something you've seen.

items.title: Behavior
items.description: What should it do? Filter, sort, update, calculate? If it just displays info, say that too.

screens.title: Try it now: describe something you actually need
steps: Think of one thing you need at work this week — a tracker, a calculator, a summary page, anything.
Open a blank note or document.
Write one paragraph describing it using the checklist: What, Who, Data, Look, Behavior.
Read it back. Would a stranger know exactly what to build? If not, add detail.
You'll use this description in 10 minutes.

screens.text: Use the APIs. Skip the pre-built workflows.
screens.sub: Practice matters. Fit matters. Building it yourself is how you learn what's possible.

screens.setup: Pre-built workflows feel productive. Someone already figured it out, right?
screens.punchline: Building your own is how you learn. Using someone else's is like reading about swimming.

screens.heading: APIs are building blocks
screens.body: Think of an API like a drive-through window. You pull up, ask for something specific, and get it handed back. You already have access to four: Slack (send and read messages), Google Drive (find and read files), Zoho Bigin (deals and contacts), and GitHub (code and projects). Claude knows how to use all of them. You just describe what you want to happen.

screens.text: Get a bad first draft fast.
screens.sub: Perfection on the first try is a myth. Speed to first draft is what matters.

steps.label: Describe
steps.detail: Write what you want using the specificity checklist. Be concrete, not perfect.

steps.label: Build
steps.detail: Give your description to Claude. Let it produce a first draft in under a minute.

steps.label: React
steps.detail: Open it. Look at it. What's wrong? What's missing? What surprised you? Trust your gut.

steps.label: Iterate
steps.detail: Tell Claude what to change. Be specific again. Each loop gets you closer.

screens.title: Pick a project, get the first draft
steps: Take the description you wrote earlier.
Paste it into Claude Code as your prompt.
Watch it build. Don't interrupt — let it finish.
Open the result in your browser.
Write down three things: one thing that's right, one thing that's wrong, one thing that's missing.

screens.heading: Local hosting — making it feel real
screens.body: Right now your project is a file on your computer. When you open it in a browser, the address bar says something like file:///C:/Users/you/project/index.html. That works, but some features need a proper web address. Local hosting means running a tiny server on your machine that gives your project a real-looking address like localhost:3000. It's still just your computer — nothing is on the internet yet. Think of it like a dress rehearsal before opening night.

screens.title: Serve it locally
screens.instruction: Ask Claude to serve your project so you can see it running like a real app.
screens.command: Can you serve this project locally so I can preview it in my browser?
screens.explanation: Claude will start a small local server and give you a link like localhost:3000. Open that link in your browser. You'll see your project running as if it were a real website. Press Ctrl+C in the terminal to stop it when you're done.

screens.text: Look at your first prompt again. Was it specific enough? What would you add now that you've seen the result?

items: Specificity is the #1 skill — use the checklist: What, Who, Data, Look, Behavior.
30 seconds of upfront thinking beats 10 minutes of back-and-forth.
Build your own workflows with APIs — don't rely on pre-built shortcuts.
Get a bad first draft fast, then iterate. Speed beats perfection.
Local hosting lets you preview your project like a real app.
screens.next: Next time: Refining a Vision — how to revise your project into something genuinely good, then ship it live to the internet.
